coredata.dc:title: Detection of Fe(III)EDTA by using photoluminescent carbon dot with the aid of F− ion
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Nan
Chai, Huijuan
Dong, Xuelin
Zhou, Qian
Zhu, Lihua
coredata.dc:description: Iron(III) ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Fe(III)EDTA) is widely used in iron fortification for reducing iron deficiency, and its determination is urgently needed. The present work developed a fluorescent method to straightforwardly determine Fe(III)EDTA by using photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) with the aid of F− ions as the masking agent of free Fe3+ ions. In the presence of F− ions, only Fe(III)EDTA selectively quenched the photoluminescence of C-dots, and both Fe3+ and Fe2+ as well as other carboxylic acids have no effect on the emission of C-dots. The sensing mechanism was attributed to the ligand-tailored electron transfer process from C-dots to Fe3+. Under optimum conditions, this method showed a linear calibration plot over the Fe(III)EDTA range of 1.0–200 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 0.4 μmol L−1. The proposed method was successfully applied to determine Fe(III)EDTA in real samples with acceptable recoveries of spikes (95%−110%) and repeatability (RSD, 4.2%−9.5%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron(III) ethylenediaminetetraacetate
Iron fortification
Carbon dots
Quenching
Electron transfer
Chelating agent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discrimination of Fe(III)EDTA and Fe3+ by using C-dots coupled with F−
Calibration curve for the detection of Fe(III)EDTA
Possible interference and analysis of real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra of C-dots in the system of (a) free Fe3+, and (d) [Fe(III)-EDTA]− in the absence and presence of chelators. Photoluminescent response of C-dots towards (b) different metal ions (100 μmol L−1), and (c) iron(III) in the absence and presence of NaF (1000 μmol L−1) and/or Na2EDTA (100 μmol L−1). The value of I 0 represented the emission intensity of C-dots (λEX = 290 nm, λEM = 410 nm) in the absence of any iron additives.
Schematic illustration of the turn off sensing mechanism of Fe(III)EDTA by using photoluminescent C-dots with the aid of F− ions as a masking agent of free Fe3+ ions.

(a) Effect of F− concentration on relative emission intensity (I/I 0) of C-dots in the presence of different concentration of free Fe3+ ions. Effect of (b) solution pH and (c) reaction time on I 0/I of C-dots in the system of (1) Fe(III)EDTA + F− and (2) Fe3+ + F−.

(a) Normalized emission spectra of C-dots with I 0 in the absence (1) and presence (2–7) of Fe(III)EDTA at pH 4. From curve 2 to 7: 0.5, 10, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μmol L−1 Fe(III)EDTA. (b) Calibration plot for determining Fe(III)EDTA by using C-dots with the aid of F− ions.

Effect of (a) metal ions, and (b) anion ions, and carboxylic acids on determination of Fe(III)EDTA (100 μmol L−1) by using C-dots with the aid of F− ions. The concentration of iron-organic complexes was 100 μmol L−1, S2−, PO4 3−, and carboxylic acids was 150 μmol L−1, and all other interferences were 500 μmol L−1, respectively.
Determination of Fe(III)EDTA in the fish sauce and iron supplement granule by using the present method (n = 5).

row.entry: Fe(III)EDTA (mmol L−1 or mg g−1) a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The unit of concentration was mmol Fe(III)EDTA per unit volume of sample A1 and A2 (mmol L−1), and mg Fe as Fe(III)EDTA per unit mass of sample B1 (mg g−1), respectively.

head.ce:title: Detection of Fe(III)EDTA by using photoluminescent carbon dot with the aid of F− ion

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Hubei Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic Chemistry and Materia Medica, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China (N. Wang; L. Zhu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic Chemistry and Materia Medica
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron(III) ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Fe(III)EDTA) is widely used in iron fortification for reducing iron deficiency, and its determination is urgently needed. The present work developed a fluorescent method to straightforwardly determine Fe(III)EDTA by using photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) with the aid of F− ions as the masking agent of free Fe3+ ions. In the presence of F− ions, only Fe(III)EDTA selectively quenched the photoluminescence of C-dots, and both Fe3+ and Fe2+ as well as other carboxylic acids have no effect on the emission of C-dots. The sensing mechanism was attributed to the ligand-tailored electron transfer process from C-dots to Fe3+. Under optimum conditions, this method showed a linear calibration plot over the Fe(III)EDTA range of 1.0–200 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 0.4 μmol L−1. The proposed method was successfully applied to determine Fe(III)EDTA in real samples with acceptable recoveries of spikes (95%−110%) and repeatability (RSD, 4.2%−9.5%).

ce:section.ce:para: Iron deficiency is the common worldwide nutritional deficiency. Food fortification programs are cost effective means for reducing the prevalence of iron deficiency (Hurrell, 1997). A number of iron forms including inorganic iron salts, elemental iron powders and iron chelates are used in the food vehicles such as rice, milk, soy sauce, fish sauce and fruit juices (Huang et al., 2009; Hurrell, Reddy, & Cook, 2000; Thuy et al., 2003; Yang, Siekmann, & Schofield, 2011). Among these iron status, iron(III) ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Fe(III)EDTA) has attracted considerable interest, because of its high stability, good solubility, and superior iron bioavailability (Huang et al., 2009; Hurrell et al., 2000; Thuy et al., 2003; Yang, et al., 2011). Hurrell et al. reported that when used to fortify foods rich in phytic acid and polyphenol, iron bioavailability from NaFeEDTA was 2–3 times higher than that of FeSO4 (Huang et al., 2009). However, high doses of iron also result in some disorders like depression, rapid and shallow respiration, coma, convulsions and cardiac arrest (Kumar et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2016).

The iron level is a critical issue in governing the functionality of iron fortified food. A variety of analytical methods, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, spectrophotometric detection using chromogenic reagents, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry have been developed to determine either Fe3+, Fe2+ or total iron (Amonette and Matya, 2015; Kumar et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2016). Since different iron species have different iron bio-accessibility, the analysis of iron speciation becomes more important. In general, the determination of iron from Fe(III)EDTA is based on the detection of either Fe3+ or EDTA2− after a dissociation (Cai, Cheng, Wu, & Yu, 2012; Kosse, Yeung, Gil, & Miller, 2001). The Fe(III)EDTA is mainly in the chelate form at pH >5, and it is totally disassembled to free Fe3+ ions at pH ≤ 0.5. By using chromogenic reagents like thiocyanate or bathophenanthroline disulfonic acid, the spectrophotometric method measures the amount of “free” Fe3+ if present in the sample at pH 5 and also the amount of total Fe3+ at pH 0.5 after a dissociation of Fe(III)EDTA (China, 2007; Kosse et al., 2001). The concentration of Fe(III)EDTA was calculated by subtracting the “free” Fe3+ at pH 5 from the total Fe3+ at pH 0.5. Nevertheless, Fe3+ ions would undergo a hydrolysis and/or a precipitation at pH 5, resulting in a great interference in the determination of “free” Fe3+ ions. Based on the determination of EDTA2− anions, Cai et al. proposed an ion chromatography (IC) method to detect NaFeEDTA in cookies (Cai et al., 2012). However, it was rather difficult to discriminate Fe(III)EDTA from other food additives like Na2EDTA and CaEDTA (Krokidis, Megoulas, & Koupparis, 2005). By combining UV detector with high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), Lucena et al. developed an ion-pairing reversed-phase HPLC method to directly determine Fe3+-chelates like EDTA in commercial fertilizers (Lucena, Barak, & Hernández-Apaolaza, 1996). The similar HPLC methods have been also proposed to determine free EDTA in feed, pharmaceutical formulations and sea water after adding Fe3+ to form Fe(III)EDTA (Chiumiento, D’Aloise, Marchegiani, & Melai, 2015; Kemmei, et al., 2012, 2013). Owing to the nature of the metal chelate equilibrium, this HPLC method requires very special elution conditions (pH, buffer and solvents). Moreover, the use of ion-pair reagents needs a considerable long time to flush out mobile phase, unless, the column lifetime would be shorten. Therefore, the identification of the same valent metal ions but at different states is still a great challenge, and the methods for the direct determination of Fe(III)EDTA are urgently needed.

Recently, fluorimetric methods based on carbon nanomaterials like carbon dots (C-dots), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and organic nanorods have been developed for imaging live cell and determining metal ions like Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ (Anand, Sivaramana, Mahesh, & Chellappa, 2015; Feng et al., 2016; Gong et al., 2015; Liang, Zhang, Zhang, & Chen, 2015; Lu et al., 2015; Maniyazagan, et al., 2018; Ranee, Sivaraman, Pushpalatha, & Muthusubramanian, 2018; Vandarkuzhali et al., 2017; Zhang, Cui, Song, Liu, & Hu, 2016; Zhou, Sheng, Han, Zou, & Li, 2012; Zhou, Zhou, Gong, Zhang, & Li, 2015). Because of the strong electron-accepting ability, Fe3+ could accept easily the electrons of photo-excited C-dots, and consequently quenched the photoluminescence (PL) of C-dots or GQDs (Feng et al., 2016; Gong et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2015). This process led to a turn-off photoluminescent sensor for the determination of Fe3+ with C-dots. Some researchers found that the oxidation of Fe2+ by H2O2 to Fe3+ also induced a photoluminescence quenching of graphene oxide, and constructed a combinational logic gate to discriminate Fe3+ and Fe2+ (Mei et al., 2012) or to determine H2O2 (Song et al., 2014). After introducing reducing reagents like ascorbic acid and chelating agents such as dopamine or F−, the quenched luminescence of C-dots by Fe3+ could be effectively recovered (Mandal et al., 2016; Qu, Wang, Ren, & Qu, 2013; Tian et al., 2017). Based on this property, the systems of C-dots and Fe3+ have been developed to determine ascorbic acid, dopamine, and F− ions (Mandal et al., 2016; Qu et al., 2013; Tian et al., 2017). However, Huang et al. found that the Fe3+-quenched emission of GQDs could not be recovered with the addition of EDTA (Huang et al., 2013). This indicates that Fe(III)EDTA can also quench the emission of C-dots. Since EDTA possesses a much stronger complexing ability (1025.1) for Fe3+ ions than F− does (1015.8), we anticipated that the addition of F− ions hardly influenced the quenched emission of C-dots by Fe(III)EDTA. Therefore, by combining photoluminescent C-dots and F− ions, the present work firstly developed a simple photoluminescent method to determine Fe(III)EDTA. This sensing system has been successfully used for the analysis of Fe(III)EDTA in iron fortified food.

Phenylboronic acid (97%) was purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). NaFeEDTA 3H2O was purchased from aladdin reagents (shanghai) Co., Ltd. Acetic acid (HOAc), sodium acetate (NaOAc), and all the other chemical reagents were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further treatments. The dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 1000) was purchased from Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout.

C-dots were synthesized with phenylboronic acid as carbon source according to the hydrothermal method with some modification (Shen et al., 2014). Briefly, 0.7 g of phenylboronic acid was dissolved in 70 mL of ultrapure water. After adjusting the solution pH to 9 by adding a 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH solution, the mixture was purged with nitrogen gas for 1 h to remove dissolved O2. Then, the reaction mixture was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heat to 160 °C for 12 h. After being cooled down to room temperature, the C-dots containing solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 24 h (molecular weight cutoff = 3500). The purified C-dots were kept at 4 °C for further use. The characterization and results were shown in Section S1, S2 and Fig. S1 of Supplementary Materials.

Unless specified otherwise, the basic reaction conditions for the determination of Fe(III)EDTA were as follows: 0.5 mL of C-dots stock dispersion (200 mg L−1), and 0.4 mL HOAc-NaOAc buffer were rapidly mixed, followed by the addition of a given volume of Fe(III)EDTA standard solution and F− ions. Then, the mixture solution was diluted to 2 mL with water. After 5 min, the emission spectra were recorded under excitation at 290 nm. Both the excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5 nm.

Two types of iron-fortified samples were analyzed including the artificial iron-fortified fish sauce and the iron supplement granule. The nonfortified fish sauce (Feng Qiu, Yi Food Co., Ltd. China) was purchased from a local market, and was used to be fortified with NaFeEDTA as the similar method in the previous literature (Thuy et al., 2003). A concentration of 4.5 mmol L−1 Fe as NaFeEDTA in the fish sauce was achieved by mixing 0.1895 g NaFeEDTA·3H2O with 100 mL fish sauce for 2 h before bottling. Prior to analysis, 4 mL fish sauce was mixed with 4 mL methanol, which helps the denaturation of proteins. The resulting mixture was shaken for 12 h at 140 rpm and 303 K in a shaking water bath. After removing protein aggregates by centrifugation at 14000 rpm for 10 min and filtration with a 0.22 μm membrane filter, the filters are diluted to 10 times with water. Then, 1 mL dilution solution was passed through a hydrophobic solid phase extraction cartridge (Bond-Elut C18 column, 500 mg, Agilent) for a further purification. The organic matrix compounds retained on the cartridge, while most fraction of Fe(III)EDTA species was eluted. The cartridge was then rinsed with 3.5 mL water, because water can elute Fe(III)EDTA, but not elute organic matrixes. When applying 1 mL of 100 μmol L−1 Fe(III)EDTA standard solution to the cartridge, it was found that almost all of the Fe(III)EDTA was eluted by 3 mL water. Therefore, 3.5 mL water should be enough to elute Fe(III)EDTA in the fish sauce. Finally, the collected eluate was diluted to 5 mL with water, followed by measurements. If needed, the samples were spiked with Fe(III)EDTA stock solution before pretreatment. The measurement processes of Fe(III)EDTA were the same as those in the section of 2.3. To further reduce the possible matrix effect of fish sauce, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence of pretreated fish sauce without Fe(III)EDTA was taken as the initial value of I 0.

The iron supplement granule was purchased from Shanghai Gold-Banded Cudgel Healthcare Product Co. Ltd, and it contained 1.5 mg g−1 Fe from NaFeEDTA. To analyze the iron supplement granule, 0.5 g sample was dissolved in 50 mL water under stirring. If needed, the samples were spiked with Fe(III)EDTA during the dissolving process. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters. The collected filtrates were directly measured by the proposed fluorescence method, because the ingredients in the iron supplement granule including sucrose, starch, Vitamin C, and citric acid exhibited no interference on the determination of Fe(III)EDTA. Shen et al. also demonstrated that sucrose and Vitamin C have very little effects on the emission of C-dots prepared by the same method (Shen et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of Fe(III)EDTA and Fe3+ by using C-dots coupled with F−
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared C-dots are approximately spherical in shape with a primary particle size of about 5.0 nm (Fig. S1a), and their number-weighted average hydrodynamic diameter is 30 nm after dispersing in pH 4.0 buffer solutions (Fig. S2a). The FTIR analysis confirms that C-dots possess abundant surface hydroxyl groups (Fig. S1b), which are helpful for the formation of complexions with metal ions. The C-dots show a broad UV–Vis absorption range with a strong peak at 250 nm and two weak shoulders around 290 and 365 nm (Fig. S1c), which are attributed to the π-π∗ transition of carbon materials, and surface defects, respectively (Song et al., 2014). When excited by UV light with wavelength less than 320 nm, the C-dots exhibited a photoluminescent (PL) emission peak centered at 410 nm (Fig. S1d). If further increasing the excitation wavelength to 460 nm, the PL emission peak gradually red-shifted to 544 nm and the intensity significantly decreased (Fig. S1d). The emission of C-dots was unchanged even in the presence of high concentration of NaCl (0.5 mol L−1) (Fig. S3), supporting that C-dots may be a potential sensor for the applications in the field of food.

Fig. 1 a illustrated the PL emission changes of C-Dots in the presence of 100 μmol L−1 Fe3+. The addition of Fe3+ ions significantly decreased the emission of C-dots, yielding a quenching ratio of 67.0%. A screening experiment demonstrated there was no significant effect on the PL intensity of C-dots in the presence of other metal ions (100 μmol L−1) including Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Al3+ (Fig. 1b). This indicated a high sensitivity of the prepared C-dots towards Fe3+ ions. However, the successive addition of the chelators like F− ions (NaF, 1000 μmol L−1) and EDTA2− (Na2EDTA, 1000 μmol L−1) affected the quenched emission of C-dots in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 1a). The introduction of F− ions led to a nearly complete luminescence recovery of the mixed C-dots and free Fe3+ system, being similar to the report of Mandal et al. (2016). In contrast, the successive addition of Na2EDTA did not recover the Fe3+-quenched emission of C-dots, but significantly enhanced the quenching effect of Fe3+ ions from 67.0% to by 84.9%. Huang et al. also found that Fe3+ ions quenched the PL emission of graphene quantum dots, and the luminescence could not be recovered by adding EDTA (Huang et al., 2013). In the control cases, the addition of Na2EDTA did not influence the emission of C-dots, but the molar equivalent NaFeEDTA showed a similar quenching degree (86.1%) to that (84.9%) in the co-existence of Fe3+ and Na2EDTA (Fig. 1c). These comparisons confirmed that the photoluminescent C-dots exhibited the specific “turn off” responses to both free Fe3+ ions and Fe(III)EDTA complexes. Interestingly, the quenching performance is dramatically diverse after introducing F− ions. As mentioned above, the addition of F− ions induced a luminescence recovery in the system of C-dots and free Fe3+ ions, but it did not influence the luminescence of the system of C-dots and Fe(III)EDTA (Fig. 1a and 1c). Thus, it was anticipated that photoluminescent C-dots can discriminate Fe(III)EDTA and free Fe3+ ions with the aid of F− ions.

To confirm this proposal, we further investigated the influence of Fe3+ on the determination of Fe(III)EDTA with C-dots. In the presence of F− ions, the mixture of Fe(III)EDTA and Fe3+ showed the same degree of influence (84.9%) on the quenched emission of C-dots as the Fe(III)EDTA alone did (86.1%) (Fig. 1d). In a control test, when no F− ions were added, the quenching ratio on the emission of C-dots caused by Fe(III)EDTA was increased from 86.1% to 94.4% in the co-existence of Fe3+ (Fig. 1d). These comparisons demonstrated that the introduction of Fe3+ into the mixture of C-dots and Fe(III)EDTA had no impact on the detection of Fe(III)EDTA in the presence of F− ions as the masking agent. Therefore, the integration of photoluminescent C-dots with F− ions could act as a “turn off” sensor for selectively determining Fe(III)EDTA.

It is generally considered that the partly filled d orbits in transition metal ions like Fe3+ make them readily accept electrons from C-dots and GQDs (Gong et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2015). Since the conduction band of C-dots is around − 0.26 V (vs. NHE) (Liu et al., 2015), and the standard redox potential is 0.77 V for Fe3+/Fe2+ and −0.037 V (vs. NHE) for Fe3+/Fe, respectively, the photo-induced single electron transfer from C-dots to Fe3+ is more thermodynamically favored, which would yield Fe2+ (Fig. 2 ). To verify this proposal, the generation of Fe2+ was determined with orto-phenantroline as a typical chromogenic agent (Moldovan, Ananeage, 2002). For example, the absorbance at 510 nm was increased to 1.43 when mixing orto-phenantroline with Fe2+, but the absorption was negligible if replacing Fe2+ by the same level of Fe3+ (Fig. S4a). After adding C-dots into the mixture of orto-phenantroline and Fe3+, the absorbance at 510 nm was almost unchanged (around 0.13) at dark, but it was gradually increased to 0.21 as increasing UV irradiation time to 30 min (Fig. S4b and S4d). Comparably, the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and orto-phenantroline in the absence of C-dots showed negligible changes upon UV irradiation (Fig. S4c and S4d). Therefore, the photo-induced single electron transfer from C-dots to Fe3+ occurred. This process causes the perturbation of electronic states of C-dots and non-radiative transitions, thereby quenching the luminescence of C-dots.

The presence of a competitive chelator could weaken the interaction between C-dots and metal ions, and then release C-dots. The Fe3+-quenched luminescence of C-dots was recovered by adding F− ions, but not by EDTA, although EDTA has much stronger complexing ability (1025.1) than F− ions (1015.8) for Fe3+ ions. This excluded the contribution of competitive interaction between chelators and C-dots. Moreover, the size distribution measurements showed that the presence of Fe3+ ions increased the hydrodynamic diameter of C-dots from 30 nm to 100 nm (Fig. S2a and S2b), suggesting the formation of C-dots clusters. After further introducing the chelators, the clusters were not liberated, but grew up to 110 and 220 nm in the presence of F−, and EDTA, respectively (Fig. S2b–d). Thus, it can be concluded that both FeF6 3− and Fe(III)EDTA could be adsorbed on the surface of C-dots, due to the well-known hydrogen bond through the NH2 group of EDTA or F− with OH groups of C-dots (Anand, Sivaramana, Iniya, Siva, & Chellappa, 2015; Kumar, Kesavan, Sivaramanc, & Rajesha, 2018; Mandal et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2014).It is worth noting that the chelating agents dramatically influence both the thermodynamics and kinetics features of the iron redox reactions (Jones, Griffin, & Waite, 2015), thereby altering its electron accepting behavior. F− is a strong Fe3+ chelator (stability constant, 1015.8) but has negligible complexing ability with Fe2+ (0.8), thereby significantly decreasing the standard redox potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ (
E
Fe
3 +
/Fe
2 +
θ
) from 0.77 V to −0.17 V (vs. NHE) (Eqs. S1–S3), and stabilizing Fe3+ from the reduction (Croot and Hunter, 2000). Therefore, the presence of F− inhibited the photo-induced electron transfer from C-dots to Fe3+ in thermodynamics (Fig. 4), leading to a luminescence recovery of C-dots. EDTA also has a larger stability constant for Fe3+ (1025.1) than that for Fe2+ (1014.3), but the stability constant ratio of Fe3+L/Fe2+L in the EDTA-complexions (1010.8) is five orders of magnitude less than that in the F−-complexions (1015.8). As a result, the complexion with EDTA changes
E
Fe
3 +
/Fe
2 +
θ
to a less negative value, i.e., 0.13 V (vs. NHE) (Eqs. S4–S6). The thermodynamic driving force of Fe(III)EDTA for accepting electrons is decreased, but the photo-induced electron transfer from C-dots to Fe(III)EDTA is still thermodynamically feasible. Previous reports have demonstrated that the addition of EDTA can accelerate significantly the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycling in the Fenton-like systems, possible due to that chelates strongly influenced the electronic states of metal ions (An, Zhu, Zhang, & Tang, 2013; Luo et al., 2009; Song et al., 2012; Wang, Zhu et al., 2011; Wang, Sun et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2012). Thus, it is rational to propose that the enhanced electron transfer rate was the main reason for the stronger quenched effect of Fe(III)EDTA on the emission of C-dots. Because of the much weaker complexing ability (1015.8) than EDTA (1025.1), F− ions could not break the Fe(III)EDTA complexes, thereby hardly changing the Fe(III)EDTA-quenched emission of C-dots. Fig. 2 illustrated the schematic representation of fluorescent C-dot with the aid of F− for determining Fe(III)EDTA.

Furthermore, other iron-chelators may be capable of accepting the photo-induced electrons of C-dots. There are another three kinds of iron-complexions as food fortifiers or additives, Fe(III)-citrate, Fe(II)-fumarate and Fe(III)NTA (2,2′,2″-nitrilotriacetic acid, NTA). Only Fe(III)-citrate quenched the emission of C-dots (Fig. S5). Like EDTA, citrate can form complexions with both iron(III) and iron(II), and the redox potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ in the Fe(III)-citrate (0.21 V, Eqs. S7–S9) was between free iron ions and Fe(III)EDTA. As a result, the photo-induced electron transfer from C-dots to Fe(III)-citrate is allowed and consequently quenched the emission of C-dots. Fe(II)-fumarate is a very weak electron acceptor, while Fe(III)NTA tends to transform into FeF6 3−, because of its lower lower complexing ability, therefore, they displayed no observable influence on the emission of C-dots. Since the plural fortifiers of two iron-chelators are very rare events, the present work can be also used to determine Fe(III)-citrate.

The combination of C-dots and F− ions provided a simple fluorescence method to identify Fe(III)EDTA and Fe3+. It should be pointed out that the C-dots alone could be used to determine Fe(III)EDTA if Fe3+ ions are absent. However, because it is uncertain whether or not free Fe3+ ions are present, it is better to add F− anion to exclude the potential interferences of Fe3+. To obtain high sensitivity for sensing Fe(III)EDTA, the reaction factors including the F− concentration, solution pH, and reaction time were optimized.

Fig. 3
a illustrated the effect of F− concentration on the emission of the mixture of C-dots and Fe3+. At any a given Fe3+ concentration, as the F− concentration was increased, the photoluminescence of C-dots was gradually recovered. In all the tested Fe3+ concentration, the quenched emission of C-dots by Fe3+ achieved a nearly complete recovery when the molar ratio of F− to Fe3+ was beyond 6, due to that the stoichiometric ratio of F−/Fe3+ in the stable FeF6 3− complexes is 6. In consideration to the maximum concentration of iron(III) no more than 200 μmol L−1, the concentration of F− ions was set as 1.2 mmol L−1.

Fig. 3b showed the effect of pH on the photoluminescence response of C-dots towards Fe3+ and Fe(III)EDTA in the presence of 1.5 mmol L−1 F−. As the solution pH was increased from 1 to 4, the quenched luminescence of C-dots in the presence of Fe3+ and F− ions was gradually recovered, and completely recovered at pH ≥ 4. At pH 1, 99% of F− is in the form of HF (pKa = 3.2), which was unfavorable to the formation of FeF6 3−. An increasing in the solution pH from 1 to 4 leads to that the distribution ratio of free F− ions was increased to 86%, which favors the formation of FeF6 3− and the consequent luminescence recovery of C-dots. In contrast, the quenching effect of Fe(III)EDTA in the presence of F− ions was independent on the solution pH in a range of pH 1–6. According to these results, the pH adjustment provides an alternative approach to identify the systems of Fe(III)EDTA, Fe3+, and their mixture. We define a parameter Δ that represents the difference of quenching efficiency between pH 1 and pH 4: Δ = [1 − (I/I 0)pH1] − [1 − (I/I 0)pH4] = (I/I 0)pH4 − (I/I 0)pH1. When Δ = 0 and (I/I 0)pH4 < 1, only Fe(III)EDTA is present; when Δ < 0 and (I/I 0)pH4 = 1, only Fe3+ is present; when Δ < 0 and (I/I 0)pH4 < 1, both Fe(III)EDTA and Fe3+ are present. To get the high selectivity of C-dots for sensing Fe(III)EDTA, the solution pH was chosen as 4.

Fig. 3c illustrated the luminescence change of C-dots in the presence of Fe(III)EDTA at different reaction time. This system reached an approximately constant value at 2 min, which was maintained for at least 30 min. Thus, the photoluminescence measurements were conducted at 5 min after mixing the reactants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve for the detection of Fe(III)EDTA
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the above general procedure, the determination conditions were optimized as F− ions concentration = 1.5 mmol L−1, reaction time = 5 min, pH = 4.0. Under optimal conditions, the concentration-dependent manner for the Fe(III)EDTA-induced emission quenching of C-dots was analyzed. As increasing the Fe(III)EDTA concentration, the quenching effect on the emission of C-dots became stronger (Fig. 4a). A modified Stern-Volmer model was utilized to obtain a calibration plot by using Eq. (1) (Wang, Zhu et al., 2011; Wang, Sun et al., 2011) [42],
(1)
ln
I
0
I
=
K
SV
′
[ Fe(III)EDTA ] + n
where I 0, and I is the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the absence and presence of quencher, K SV’ is the modified Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and n is a constant, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, the value of ln(I 0/I) was linearly dependent on the concentration of Fe(III)EDTA over the range of 1.0–200 μmol L−1 with a regression equation of ln[I 0/I] = 0.0185[Fe(III)EDTA] + 0.0282 (R2 = 0.9957). The detection limit was estimated from the mean signal of the blank and three times the standard deviation of the blank. The blank sample consisted of all the same reagents and solvent but without any iron additives. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.4 μmol L−1 (3σ of the reagent blank signal). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 11 replicate measurements for 5 and 100 μmol L−1 Fe(III)EDTA were 4.5%, and 5.0%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible interference and analysis of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Possible interferences from metal ions, anion ions, and carboxylic acids were checked. It was found that all the tested metal ions including Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ at a concentration of 500 μmol L−1 exhibited no significant effect on the determination of Fe(III)EDTA with C-dots in the presence of F− (Fig. 5 a), because all these cations have a much lower stability constant (107.32−1023.4) toward EDTA than Fe3+ ions (1025.1). The tested anion ions such as NO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, S2O3 2−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, S2− and PO4 3−, and carboxylic acids like oxalic, citric acid and 2,2′,2″-nitrilotriacetic acid showed weak effect on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots (Fig. 5b). This is probably due to the fact that EDTA has stronger affinity toward Fe3+ than the tested anions and carboxylic acids.

The proposed fluorescent method was further applied to determine Fe(III)EDTA in the real samples. The detected Fe(III)EDTA in the unfortified fish sauce (i.e., sample A1) was below the detection limit of this method, and it was 4.1 mmol L−1 in the fortified sample A2 and 21.45 mg g−1 Fe as NaFeEDTA in the iron supplement granule (i.e., sample B1) (Table 1 ). Recoveries were measured by spiking the above samples with a certain amount of Fe(III)EDTA, and subjected to the same pre-treatments and measurements. The recovery of spikes was calculated from the following equation:
(2)
Recovery =
Found - Blank
Added
× 100 %
where Blank is the measured amount of Fe(III)EDTA in samples before spiking, Found and Added is the measured and added amount of Fe(III)EDTA in spiked samples. The obtained mean recoveries were between 95.0% and 110.0%, and the RSD of replicate measurements was less than 10% (n = 5, Table 1). These results indicated that the proposed method provides an alternative strategy for determining Fe(III)EDTA in fish sauce and iron supplement granule. Moreover, the present sensor has been applied to detect Fe(III)EDTA in other food vehicles such as soy sauce and biscuit. We found that the co-existed matrix showed either absorption or fluorescence at 280–450 nm, which influenced the emission of C-dots and consequently impacted the determination of Fe(III)EDTA. To avoid the background fluorescence interference of substrates, it is better to develop longer exciting and emitting sensing systems of C-dots.

This work developed a new and simple fluorescent method to determine Fe(III)EDTA by the combination of C-dots with F− ions. It was confirmed that it was Fe(III)EDTA but not free Fe3+ ions selectively quenched the luminescence of C-dots in the presence of F− ions, because F− ions could completely mask the possible interference of free Fe3+ ions. This method could detect Fe(III)EDTA within 5 min, and it provided a working range of 1.0–200 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 0.4 μmol L−1. There was little or no interference from various cations, anions and carboxylic acids, because Fe(III)EDTA is a very stable complexes among various Fe3+-ligands and metal-EDTA complexes. The analysis of real samples yielded acceptable recoveries ranging from 95% to 110%. The proposed method provides an alternative methodology for designing fluorescent assays to straightforwardly determine Fe(III)EDTA.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21477043). We would like to thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for the technical support in the materials characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2018.03.050.
